short.headline: India started even, but fell behind Asia's tigers, here's exactly where and why.
short.dek: Seven decades of data on health, schooling, investment, and factories explain the great divergence.
short.body: In 1950, India was no poorer than China or South Korea. But while East Asia followed a sequence of building human capital, saving heavily, and moving into manufacturing, India underdid each step. The result is visible in every comparison: life expectancy, schooling, women's work, factory share, and productivity. Yet India crushed extreme poverty and did so as a continuous democracy. The data tells a story not of absolute failure, but of a different, slower path with its own trade-offs.

macha.heading: So what's the real story, macha?
macha.body: Think of it this way. In 1950, India and South Korea were on the same starting line. But while Korea and China poured money into health, schools, factories, and tech, India moved slower. Korea built a manufacturing powerhouse; India's factories stayed small. Korea's women joined the workforce; India's largely stayed out. The result? A Korean worker now produces in an hour what an Indian produces in six. But here's the twist: India still crushed extreme poverty, built a world-class services sector, and did it all as a loud, messy democracy. The East Asian path was faster but often brutal. India's path is slower but gentler. The numbers don't pick a winner; they show the trade-offs.
macha.soWhat: India's journey isn't a failure — it's a different, more democratic route that still delivered big wins, but left money on the table.

article.title: Why did India stay poor while the rest of Asia got rich?
article.standfirst: Around 1950 India was no poorer than China or South Korea. Seventy years of data on human capital, investment, and manufacturing explain why the gap opened, and why the democratic route matters.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## Weren't India and China equally poor to begin with?
In 1950, the average Indian was no poorer than the average Chinese or South Korean. The Maddison Project's long-run reconstruction puts India's GDP per person at about $990 that year, slightly above China's roughly $800 and close to South Korea's about $1,000. Bangladesh was at about $860. Yet by 2022, the same series shows India at $7,766, while China reached $19,238 and South Korea soared to $41,321. The divergence started in the 1970s and widened dramatically. Nothing about 1950 explains that. The explanation is in everything that happened next.

## How big is the income gap now?
Measured at purchasing-power parity in constant 2021 dollars, India's GDP per capita in 2024 was $9,818. China's stood at $23,846, South Korea's $55,071, Vietnam's $14,415, and Bangladesh's $8,487. The world average was $21,405. India's line rose, especially after 1990, but others rose faster. The PPP adjustment makes the gap smaller than market rates would, but the ranking remains: India lags behind.

## How long did people live in India compared to its peers?
In 1960, India's life expectancy at birth was 45.6 years. By 2024 it had risen to 72.2, a huge gain. But China reached 78.0, South Korea 83.6, Vietnam 74.7, and Bangladesh 74.9. East Asia invested in public health early, opening a life-expectancy gap decades before the income gap fully widened.

## How many children survived to age five?
In 1960, India's under-five mortality was 241.3 per 1,000 live births. By 2024 it dropped to 26.6. Yet China had only 5.7, South Korea 2.8, Vietnam 17.3, and Bangladesh 30.5. India's child survival improved, but for decades remained worse than its peers. This lag in early-life health is one deep root of the human-capital gap.

## How many years did an average person spend in school?
Adults in India averaged 0.16 years of school in 1900. By 2020, that was 7.8 years. China reached 9.0, South Korea 13.7, Taiwan 12.8, and Japan 12.8. Bangladesh had 7.2. East Asia pulled ahead in schooling stock by the 1980s, giving its workforce the skills to run complex factories.

## Where are India's women in the workforce?
Only 32.4% of working-age Indian women were in the labour force in 2025. China's rate was 59.1%, South Korea's 56.8%, Vietnam's 68.6%, and Bangladesh's 38.6%. The world average was 48.9%. India's rate has stagnated for decades and is an extreme outlier. Much of women's work is undercounted, but the gap remains vast, limiting the effective workforce.

## How fast did families shrink?
India's fertility rate fell from 5.9 births per woman in 1960 to 2.0 in 2024. China's dropped to 1.0, South Korea's to 0.7, Vietnam's to 1.9, and Bangladesh's to 2.1. East Asia's faster decline opened a demographic window with fewer dependents per worker, which fuelled growth.

## How many children are stunted?
35.5% of Indian under-fives are stunted, a sign of chronic malnutrition. That is worse than Bangladesh's 23.6%, Indonesia's 22%, Vietnam's 18.2%, and China's 4.8%. India's stunting rate is high even compared to poorer countries, indicating deep-rooted problems in nutrition and sanitation.

## How much did India invest in building its future?
Gross fixed capital formation, the investment rate, was 14.5% of GDP in India in 1960. It rose to 29.9% by 2024. But China invested 39.9%, South Korea 30.0%, and Vietnam 29.0%. India's investment rate was lower for decades, meaning it built less infrastructure and machinery per unit of output.

## Who saved to pay for all that investment?
India's gross savings rate was 13.1% in 1975, rising to 30.3% in 2024. China saved 42.8%, South Korea 35.0%, Vietnam 36.7%, and the world 26.2%. The gap in savings mirrored the investment gap; East Asia funded its construction boom through high domestic thrift.

## Did foreign money come in to build factories?
Foreign direct investment as a share of GDP was 0.7% in India in 2024, and similarly low for South Korea and China. But Vietnam received 4.2%. In the critical early years, India's FDI was negligible, and it missed the technology transfer that export-oriented FDI brought to Vietnam and earlier to China.

## Why didn't India build a big manufacturing sector?
India's manufacturing value added has barely changed: 14.8% of GDP in 1960, 12.6% in 2024. South Korea went from 11.4% to 26.6%, Thailand from 12.5% to 24.3%, Malaysia from 10.3% to 22.5%, Bangladesh from 5.3% to 21.9%. India's factory sector stayed small, missing the escalator that mass manufactures provided.

## Did India skip the factory and jump to services?
Agriculture fell from 41.7% to 16.3% of GDP between 1960 and 2024. Services rose from 38.8% to 49.9%, but industry only moved from 20.8% to 24.6%. India's growth path bypassed large-scale industrialisation, with services leading the way, a pattern that skipped the labour-absorbing factory stage.

## When did workers leave the farm?
In 1991, 63.1% of Indian workers were in agriculture. By 2025, that fell to 41.6%. In the same period, China's fell from 59.6% to 21.7%, Vietnam's from 74.0% to 25.0%, and Bangladesh's from 69.9% to 44.3%. India's movement out of farming was slower, leaving too many workers in low-productivity jobs.

## How sophisticated are India's exports?
The Economic Complexity Index for India rose from 0.38 in 1995 to 0.71 in 2024. But South Korea reached 1.60, China 1.27, Thailand 0.81, and Vietnam surged from -0.97 to 0.67. India's export basket remained less diverse and lower-tech than the East Asian winners.

## What does India sell to the world?
India's manufactured exports were 43.4% of merchandise exports in 1962, rising to 67.1% in 2024. South Korea reached 87.3%, China 91.2%, Vietnam 85.0%, and Bangladesh 94.5%. India's export mix remained less concentrated in manufactures, relying more on raw materials and fuels.

## How much does an hour of Indian work produce?
Output per hour worked was $1.86 in India in 1970, reaching $8.06 in 2023. China's output hit $17.69, South Korea's $53.61, Taiwan's $60.85, and Vietnam's $11.03. The productivity gap explains the income gap: an Indian worker produces far less per hour because of lower capital and less advanced industry.

## How much does India spend on inventing the future?
India's R&D expenditure has stayed at about 0.6% of GDP from 1996 to 2020. South Korea rose from 2.1% to 4.9%, China from 0.6% to 2.6%, and Japan from 2.6% to 3.4%. India's flat investment in innovation suggests it is not building the technological base for rapid upgrading.

## How much electricity does India use?
Electricity use per person was 271 kWh in India in 1990 and 1,182 kWh in 2023. South Korea used 11,350 kWh, China 6,524 kWh, and Vietnam 2,585 kWh. Low electricity consumption reflects lower industrialisation and household access, a physical measure of the development gap.

## How many Indians live in cities?
Urbanisation rose from 17.9% in 1960 to 35.4% in 2024 in India. South Korea reached 81.2%, China 65.9%, and Vietnam 38.5%. India's slower urbanisation means fewer workers concentrate in high-productivity city jobs.

## Did India actually reduce poverty?
India's extreme poverty ($3/day) fell from 59.7% in 1977 to just 5.3% in 2022. This is a remarkable decline, comparable to China's from 97.0% to 0% and Vietnam's from 57.5% to 1.6%. India lifted hundreds of millions out of destitution, even without a manufacturing boom.

## What is India's overall human development score?
The Human Development Index rose from 0.45 in 1990 to 0.69 in 2023 for India. China reached 0.80, South Korea 0.94, Vietnam 0.77, and Bangladesh tied India at 0.69. The composite shows India behind, with lower-than-expected health and education components.

## Was India's path democratic while others weren't?
India's electoral democracy index was 0.38 in 2025, constant since independence. China's was 0.07, autocratic. South Korea (0.82) and Taiwan (0.80) democratised only in the late 1980s, after their growth takeoffs. India's democracy, with its constraints, likely came with an economic cost, but also avoided the coercion seen elsewhere.

## How should you read these comparisons?
Sources: Maddison Project for long-run GDP (with wide error bars pre-1950); World Bank for PPP and most indicators (modelled); Penn World Table for productivity; Harvard Atlas for ECI; UNDP for HDI; V-Dem for democracy. Pre-1950 figures are approximate; PPP is modelled, not market rates; Taiwan is absent from some datasets; China's manufacturing series starts in 2004. These charts show associations, not proven causes. East Asia followed an integrated model; India underdid each step, under the real constraint of democracy.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: A striking set of charts that together answer why India fell behind East Asia after starting from the same line around 1950. The story moves through human capital, female labour force drop-out, weak investment, skipped manufacturing, low export complexity, and resulting productivity gaps, before acknowledging poverty reduction, human development gains, and the democratic constraint.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Picture dividing the entire economic output of India among all its people. That is GDP per capita — the average value of goods and services produced per person in a year. It is the most common yardstick for a country's material standard of living. High does not mean equal, but it means the average pot is bigger. It excludes unpaid work like caregiving and does not tell you how the income is shared.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: GDP per capita is the headline number that shows how far India's average income lags behind East Asian comparators, and how that gap opened over decades.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A rupee buys more in India than a dollar buys in the United States for the same basket of haircuts, rotis, and rent. PPP adjusts for that: it re-prices every country's output as if a dollar bought the same amount everywhere. This gives a fairer comparison of real living standards than market exchange rates. But it is a modelled adjustment, not a market fact.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: All modern income comparisons in this page use constant PPP dollars, so the gaps you see are in real purchasing power, not distorted by currency swings.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Human capital is the stock of health, skills, and knowledge embodied in a country's people. It is what lets a worker be trained, show up healthy, and be productive. It is built through nutrition, healthcare, and schooling, starting from a child's first thousand days. It is not money in the bank; it is a nation's collective capability.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: This page argues that East Asia built human capital before and during its income takeoff, while India lagged on health and education, and that lag shows up in nearly every chart.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This is the share of women aged 15 and older who are either working for pay or actively looking for work. It counts only measured, market-based work. It misses the enormous amount of unpaid household and care work that women do. So a low number does not mean women are idle; it means their work is not counted in the formal economy.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India's rate is an extreme outlier among Asian comparators. The ILO modelled estimates used here may differ from India's own surveys, and much of women's work is invisible in these statistics. Still, the gap is real and means a smaller effective workforce.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This is the part of a country's output that is ploughed back into building long-term productive assets: factories, machines, roads, power plants, and buildings. It is the 'investment' share of GDP. Without it, an economy consumes its seed corn and cannot grow its future capacity.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: East Asia ran investment rates far above India's for decades, which is the under-told engine of their growth. This chart measures that gap.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: It is the share of GDP that comes from making physical goods in factories — cars, textiles, steel, and electronics. Manufacturing is historically the ladder that poor countries climb, because it is far more productive than farming and can absorb millions of workers. A falling share does not always mean deindustrialisation, but in India's case it indicates a sector that never took off.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India's manufacturing share has barely moved in sixty years, while South Korea's soared. This is at the heart of the structural divergence.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Developed by Harvard's Growth Lab, it ranks countries by how diverse and sophisticated their export basket is. A high score means a country makes and sells many different products that few other countries can produce. It is a measure of productive knowledge, not just trade volume.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: This index shows that East Asian economies climbed the complexity ladder rapidly while India's basket remained simpler, a sign of weaker industrial and technological depth.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The HDI, from the United Nations, combines life expectancy, education, and income into one number between 0 and 1. It is a rough all-in scorecard of how a society is doing beyond just money. A high income alone does not guarantee a high HDI if health and schooling lag.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: This index sums up the broader gap: India's income is higher than some peers', yet its HDI is lower because health and education outcomes are weaker.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Produced by the V-Dem project, this index measures how free and fair a country's elections are, scoring from 0 (autocracy) to 1 (full democracy). It captures whether leaders are chosen through competitive votes with broad suffrage, not whether governance is good or civil liberties are perfect.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It shows that India has been a continuous electoral democracy since independence, while South Korea and Taiwan democratised only after their growth takeoffs. That political choice is an essential part of the comparison.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A child is stunted if their height is far below the healthy average for their age. It signals chronic malnutrition in early life, which also scars brain development. Stunting is a physical ledger of a society's failure to feed and care for its youngest. It is irreversible after a certain age, so a high rate means a generation of children who will never reach their full physical or cognitive potential.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India's stunting rate is bafflingly high for its income level, even compared to much poorer countries, pointing to deep failures in nutrition, sanitation, and care that undermine future human capital.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The total fertility rate is the average number of children a woman would have over her lifetime if current birth patterns held. A rate of about 2.1 is considered replacement level — the population stabilises. Below that, it shrinks in the long run without migration. It is a powerful signal of women's education, access to contraception, and economic roles.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: East Asia's faster fertility decline opened a demographic window earlier, with more workers per child, while India's slower decline kept dependency ratios higher for longer.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This is the number of children out of every 1,000 live births who die before reaching age five. It is a razor-sharp proxy for a country's public health, nutrition, and water quality, because young children are the most vulnerable to disease and deprivation. A high rate means the most basic protective systems are failing.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India's rate has fallen dramatically but remains above many Asian comparators, showing that child health arrived later and less completely.

chartExplainers.title: The same starting line, and the great divergence
chartExplainers.takeaway: Around 1950, India, China, and South Korea had similar income levels, but by 2022, South Korea was five times richer than India.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart plots GDP per capita from 1900 to 2022 using the Maddison Project's long-run reconstruction. In the early 20th century, all lines are clustered below $1,000. After 1950, they begin to spread. South Korea takes off dramatically after the 1960s, China after the 1980s. India's line rises, but far more slowly. By 2022, South Korea hit $41,321, China $19,238, and India $7,766. Indonesia reached $12,802, and Bangladesh $4,926. The key insight is the timing of divergence: it intensifies exactly when East Asian states invested in education, health, and manufacturing.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This chart sets up the puzzle by showing the common starting point and the vast gap that opened.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the fan shape after 1960: the lines separate vertically as some countries grow much faster.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read pre-1950 levels as precise. They are approximations with wide error bars.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Focus on the relative trajectories, not exact numbers. Single-color lines with clear labels work best.

chartExplainers.title: The income gap, measured
chartExplainers.takeaway: In 2024, India's GDP per capita at PPP was $9,818, while South Korea's was $55,071.
chartExplainers.detail: Using constant 2021 international dollars, this chart shows the modern, like-for-like comparison. India's line rises from $2,203 in 1990 to $9,818 in 2024. But China jumped from $1,667 to $23,846, and South Korea from $14,378 to $55,071 over the same period. Vietnam started at $2,468 and reached $14,415. Bangladesh climbed from $2,070 to $8,487. The world average rose from $11,257 to $21,405. India's post-1991 reforms accelerated growth, but the gap with the fastest growers did not close.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The PPP measure strips out price differences and gives the most honest real-income comparison.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Pay attention to the vertical spacing between the lines at the right edge — that is the current gap.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not compare these PPP numbers with market-exchange-rate dollar figures.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a single y-axis with each country in a distinct colour; avoid clutter.

chartExplainers.title: How long people live
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's life expectancy rose from 45.6 to 72.2 years, but South Korea reached 83.6 and Bangladesh 74.9.
chartExplainers.detail: Life expectancy at birth is graphed from 1960. India's line moves from 45.6 to 72.2 years by 2024. China, after a low of 33.4 in 1960, climbed to 78.0. South Korea went from 53.8 to 83.6. Vietnam progressed from 58.0 to 74.7, and Bangladesh from 44.0 to 74.9. The lines converge somewhat, but India's remained lower than its East Asian peers for the entire period. The chart shows that the health gap opened early: by 1980, South Korea had already reached 65, while India was at 55.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Health is the foundation of human capital, and this early divergence set the stage for later income gaps.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Watch how the lines separate in the 1960s-80s, then India's gradual catch-up.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume this is just about doctors; public health, sanitation, and nutrition matter more.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Smooth line chart with a few labelled years.

chartExplainers.title: Children who don't reach five
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's under-five mortality fell from 241 to 26.6 per 1,000, but South Korea's is now 2.8.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart tracks deaths per 1,000 live births from 1960. India's rate dropped from 241.3 to 26.6 in 2024. China's descent was steeper: from 118 in 1969 to 5.7. South Korea went from 112.9 to 2.8, Vietnam from 99.4 to 17.3, Bangladesh from 263.6 to 30.5. India's line stayed above most Asian lines throughout. This means that for decades, a child born in India was more likely to die before age five than a child in East Asia. The gap has narrowed recently but remains large.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Child survival is the most sensitive indicator of a society's ability to protect its most vulnerable.
chartExplainers.howToRead: All lines fall; the gap is the vertical distance between them at any year.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: A low rate now doesn't erase the lasting effect of high past rates on the adult workforce.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use log scale option to show early high levels and recent low levels clearly.

chartExplainers.title: Years of schooling, the long view
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's adults averaged 7.8 years of school in 2020, while South Korea's averaged 13.7.
chartExplainers.detail: Mean years of schooling for the population aged 15+ is plotted from 1900. India started at 0.16, China at 0.02, South Korea at 0.3, Taiwan at 0.02, Japan at 1.85, and Bangladesh at 0.06. By 2020, India reached 7.8, China 9.0, South Korea 13.7, Taiwan 12.8, Japan 12.8, and Bangladesh 7.2. The East Asian lines slope upward much more steeply from the 1950s onward. India caught up in the early 20th century but fell behind after 1960. This stock of education is what industries draw upon.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Schooling is the human-capital stock that a country brings to industrialisation.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Focus on the slope of each line after 1950; steeper means faster educational accumulation.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Years of schooling is not the same as quality of learning; it's a quantity measure.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Show every 20 years for early period, then every 10 years, with labels.

chartExplainers.title: Where are the women at work?
chartExplainers.takeaway: Only 32.4% of Indian women were in the labour force in 2025, far below Vietnam's 68.6%.
chartExplainers.detail: Female labour force participation rates from 1990 to 2025 are shown. India's rate started at 30.3% and barely moved, ending at 32.4%. China fell from 73.1% to 59.1%, still high. South Korea rose from 47.2% to 56.8%. Vietnam stayed around 68-75%, ending at 68.6%. Bangladesh rose from 24.2% to 38.6%. The world average declined slightly from 51.2% to 48.9%. India's line is dramatically lower and flatter than any other Asian economy plotted.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: India's women's workforce participation is an extreme outlier and a critical drag on labour supply.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the vertical spread: India's line sits far below, barely moving.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: These are ILO modelled estimates and may differ from national surveys; much informal work is uncounted.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Keep the line simple, with annotation highlighting the gap.

chartExplainers.title: How fast families shrank
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's fertility dropped from 5.9 to 2.0 births per woman, but South Korea's crashed to 0.7.
chartExplainers.detail: Total fertility rates from 1960 to 2024 are plotted. All countries start high: India at 5.9, China 4.5, South Korea 6.0, Vietnam 6.3, Bangladesh 6.7. By 2024, India's rate was 2.0, China's 1.0, South Korea's 0.7, Vietnam's 1.9, and Bangladesh's 2.1. The lines for East Asia fall faster and earlier. South Korea's rate dipped below 3 in the mid-1970s, while India's stayed above 4 until the 1990s. This earlier decline gave East Asia a 'demographic dividend' with more workers per child.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Faster fertility decline opened a demographic window that East Asia exploited for growth.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The steepness of the line indicates the speed of transition.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Lower fertility does not automatically cause growth; it works through the age structure and if jobs exist.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line chart with the replacement-level line at 2.1 for reference.

chartExplainers.title: Children too short for their age
chartExplainers.takeaway: 35.5% of Indian children under five are stunted, nearly double Vietnam's 18.2%.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the latest available stunting rates for selected Asian countries. India stands at 35.5% (2020), Pakistan 37.6% (2018), Bangladesh 23.6% (2022), Indonesia 22% (2023), Vietnam 18.2% (2023), and China just 4.8% (2017). India's bar is among the tallest, despite being richer than several comparators. Stunting reflects chronic undernutrition and repeated infections, impairing both physical and cognitive development. The high rate is a red flag for future human capital.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Stunting is malnutrition written into a child's body and brain, and India's rate is a genuine puzzle.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the height of each bar; higher means worse malnutrition.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not interpret this as simply a lack of food; it's about overall care, sanitation, and women's health.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars work best on small screens.

chartExplainers.title: How much each country built
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's investment rate reached 29.9% in 2024, but China's has been above 30% for decades.
chartExplainers.detail: Gross fixed capital formation as a share of GDP is plotted from 1960. India started at 14.5% and climbed to 29.9% in 2024. China began at an extraordinary 32.6% and ended at 39.9%. South Korea rose from 11.2% to 30.0%. Vietnam's rate was 25.4% in 1995 and 29.0% in 2024. Japan, once a high investor, fell from 38.9% in 1970 to 26.1%. The world average was 24.1% in 1970 and 25.6% in 2024. India's line is consistently lower than China's and South Korea's peak years.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Investment is the engine of growth; East Asia poured a larger share of output into productive capacity.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Watch the vertical positions: India's line hovers around 25-30%, while China's stays above 30-40%.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: A high investment rate doesn't guarantee efficient use; quality matters too.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Sparse yearly ticks with annotations at key turning points.

chartExplainers.title: Who saved to pay for it
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's savings rate rose to 30.3% in 2024, but China's was 42.8%.
chartExplainers.detail: Gross savings as a share of GDP from the mid-1970s onward. India saved 13.1% in 1975 and reached 30.3% by 2024. China saved 33.7% in 1982 and jumped to 42.8% in 2024. South Korea saved 25.8% in 1976 and 35.0% in 2024. Vietnam saved 21.2% in 1996 and 36.7% in 2024. The world average was 22.9% in 1981 and 26.2% in 2024. The lines show that high investment in East Asia was matched by high domestic savings, making the growth largely self-financed.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Savings funded East Asia's investment boom, while India's lower savings meant less domestic capital.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the gap between India's line and the East Asian lines, especially China.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Savings include both household and corporate savings, not just personal thrift.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Keep the y-axis starting from 0 to avoid exaggerating the gap.

chartExplainers.title: Foreign money coming in
chartExplainers.takeaway: Vietnam attracted FDI of 4.2% of GDP in 2024, India just 0.7%.
chartExplainers.detail: Foreign direct investment net inflows as a share of GDP from 1970. India's inflows have been modest: 0.1% in 1970, peaking around 3.6% in 2008, and 0.7% in 2024. China's FDI was negligible until the 1990s, then surged to over 6% in 1993-94 before falling to 0.1% in 2024. South Korea has been around 0.7%. Vietnam's inflows rose sharply since the mid-2000s, reaching 4.2% in 2024. Bangladesh received 0.3%. The chart shows that Vietnam and China, at key moments, drew massive foreign capital that brought technology and export links.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: FDI can transfer technology and market access; India missed the big wave that hit Vietnam and China.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look for spikes: Vietnam's line climbs steeply after 2005.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: FDI as a percentage of GDP can look small even for large absolute inflows if GDP is big.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line chart with a note about the scale.

chartExplainers.title: The factory escalator India never rode
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's manufacturing share of GDP was 14.8% in 1960 and 12.6% in 2024, while South Korea's reached 26.6%.
chartExplainers.detail: Manufacturing value added as a share of GDP from 1960. India started at 14.8% and ended at 12.6%, with little variation. South Korea began at 11.4% and soared to a peak of 28% in the late 1980s, ending at 26.6% in 2024. Thailand rose from 12.5% to 24.3%, Malaysia from 10.3% to 22.5%. Bangladesh, from a low base of 5.3%, climbed to 21.9% driven by garments. India's line is nearly flat, demonstrating premature deindustrialisation: manufacturing never became the growth engine.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Manufacturing is the classic escalator out of poverty; India skipped it.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Note the humps for East Asia versus India's horizontal line.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: China's series starts only in 2004, so it is excluded from this chart.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a clear line chart with India's line in bold for contrast.

chartExplainers.title: India's leap from farm to office
chartExplainers.takeaway: Agriculture fell from 41.7% to 16.3% of GDP, while services rose to 49.9%, bypassing industry.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart plots India's sectoral shares from 1960. Agriculture value added declined from 41.7% to 16.3% in 2024. Industry (including manufacturing and construction) moved from 20.8% to 24.6%. Manufacturing alone was 14.8% in 1960 and 12.6% in 2024. Services rose from 38.8% to 49.9%. The crossover happened in the early 1990s, when services surpassed agriculture. Unlike the classic pattern where industry peaks above 35%, India's industry share barely budged, and services absorbed the transition.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Shows the unique structural transformation that skipped mass manufacturing.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Watch the gap between the services line and the industry + manufacturing lines widen.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Services include both high-end IT and low-end informal work; the chart doesn't distinguish.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use four distinct colours and label the agriculture and services lines clearly.

chartExplainers.title: Who left the farm, and when
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's share of workers in agriculture fell from 63.1% to 41.6%, but Vietnam's fell from 74% to 25%.
chartExplainers.detail: Employment in agriculture as a share of total employment from 1991. India started at 63.1% and dropped to 41.6% by 2025. China fell from 59.6% to 21.7%. South Korea, already low at 15.5%, went to 5.1%. Vietnam plunged from 74.0% to 25.0%. Bangladesh declined from 69.9% to 44.3%. India's descent is slower, and in 2025 it had the highest share of agricultural workers among these peers except Bangladesh. This slow shift reflects the lack of factory jobs to pull workers out of farming.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Moving workers off the farm into higher-productivity work is the essence of development.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the slopes: steeper means faster structural change.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Employment shares are modelled ILO estimates and can diverge from national censuses.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Line chart with every fifth year labelled.

chartExplainers.title: Climbing the complexity ladder
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's Economic Complexity Index rose from 0.38 to 0.71, but South Korea's reached 1.60.
chartExplainers.detail: The ECI, measuring export diversity and sophistication, from 1995 to 2024. India started at 0.38 and reached 0.71. China went from 0.63 to 1.27. South Korea from 1.10 to 1.60. Thailand from 0.41 to 0.81. Vietnam, remarkably, from -0.97 to 0.67, nearly catching India. The lines fan out, with Korea and China pulling far ahead. India's slow climb indicates its export basket remains less complex, dominated by commodities and lower-tech goods.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Complex exports drive higher incomes; India's lack of complexity is a binding constraint.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Vertical position matters: higher ECI means more sophisticated exports.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: ECI is a ranking-based metric; small changes can reflect a big shift in relative position.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Line chart with India and Vietnam highlighted to show convergence.

chartExplainers.title: Making things to sell the world
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's manufactured exports were 67.1% of merchandise exports in 2024, compared to South Korea's 87.3%.
chartExplainers.detail: Manufactures as a share of merchandise exports from the 1960s. India's share rose from 43.4% in 1962 to 67.1% in 2024. South Korea's soared from 18.2% to 87.3%. China's from 47.7% in 1984 to 91.2%. Vietnam's from 44% in 1997 to 85.0% in 2023. Bangladesh's from 57.3% to 94.5% in 2018. India's line, while rising, stays significantly below the nearly universal manufacturing dominance of East Asian exports.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Export-led growth requires selling manufactures; India leaned less on them.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The vertical gaps at the right edge show the current difference.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is share of merchandise trade only, not of total exports including services.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Line chart with annotations for the latest values.

chartExplainers.title: How much each worker produces
chartExplainers.takeaway: An Indian worker produced $8.06 per hour in 2023, a South Korean worker $53.61.
chartExplainers.detail: Output per hour worked in international dollars from 1970. India started at $1.86 in 1970 and reached $8.06 in 2023. China began at $0.69 and jumped to $17.69. South Korea went from $2.81 to $53.61. Taiwan from $8.29 to $60.85. Vietnam from $1.24 in 1983 to $11.03. Japan from $12.50 to $49.00. The lines diverge massively: the East Asian workers became many times more productive. This is the fundamental reason for the income gap.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The income gap is, at bottom, a productivity gap.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The vertical spread in 2023 is the productivity gap; a Korean hour produces 6.6 Indian hours.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Productivity is not about individual effort but about the capital and technology available.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Line chart with a log scale option to show early convergence and later divergence.

chartExplainers.title: Spending on inventing the future
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's R&D spending has been stuck at 0.6% of GDP, while South Korea's hit 4.9%.
chartExplainers.detail: Gross R&D expenditure as a share of GDP from the mid-1990s. India spent 0.6% in 1996 and 0.6% in 2020. China rose from 0.6% to 2.6% by 2023. South Korea soared from 2.1% to a world-leading 4.9%. Japan moved from 2.6% to 3.4%. India's line is a flatline at the bottom. This low research intensity means fewer innovations, less technology absorption, and a weaker pipeline for climbing the complexity ladder.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: R&D fuels future productivity; India's flat spending signals a missing upgrade engine.
chartExplainers.howToRead: India's flat line contrasts with the steep upward slopes of Korea and China.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: R&D spending alone doesn't guarantee results, but its chronic low level is a structural weakness.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Line chart with a break for Korea's high value if needed.

chartExplainers.title: The power to run a factory
chartExplainers.takeaway: India used 1,182 kWh per person in 2023, while South Korea used 11,350 kWh.
chartExplainers.detail: Electricity consumption per capita from 1990. India rose from 271 kWh to 1,182 kWh in 2023. China jumped from 511 kWh to 6,524 kWh. South Korea from 2,462 kWh to 11,350 kWh in 2024. Vietnam from 99 kWh to 2,585 kWh. The world average moved from 2,093 kWh to 3,558 kWh. India's use remains far below industrialised comparators, reflecting a smaller industrial base and limited household access. Power is a physical proxy for modern economic activity.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Electricity use tracks industrialisation and comfort; the gap is vast.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The log-scale spread shows orders of magnitude differences.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Consumption is not generation; some countries import or export electricity.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Line chart with simplified axes and large labels.

chartExplainers.title: Moving to the city
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's urban share reached 35.4% in 2024, while South Korea's is 81.2%.
chartExplainers.detail: Urban population as a share of total from 1960. India started at 17.9% and climbed to 35.4% in 2024. China went from 19.7% to 65.9%. South Korea from 27.7% to 81.2%. Vietnam from 15.3% to 38.5%. The world rose from 34.2% to 57.6%. India's urbanisation is slower and lower, meaning fewer people concentrate in cities where productivity and service delivery are higher. The classic path to development involves urbanisation alongside industrialisation.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Cities are engines of productivity; India's slower urbanisation reflects its structural path.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the slopes and the 2024 positions.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Urban definitions vary by country; India's may undercount peri-urban areas.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Simple line chart with five labelled decades.

chartExplainers.title: The poverty India did crush
chartExplainers.takeaway: Extreme poverty ($3/day) fell from 59.7% in 1977 to just 5.3% in 2022 in India.
chartExplainers.detail: Share of population living below $3 a day (2011 PPP). India's rate plummeted from 59.7% in 1977 to 5.3% in 2022. China fell from 97.0% in 1981 to 0% in 2022. Indonesia from 86.2% in 1984 to 4.0% in 2025. Vietnam from 57.5% in 1992 to 1.6% in 2022. Bangladesh from 47.1% in 1983 to 5.9% in 2022. India's decline is among the steepest, a genuine achievement. This happened despite the manufacturing lag, driven by services, remittances, and transfers.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Acknowledges the real progress: the floor rose for hundreds of millions.
chartExplainers.howToRead: All lines fall; the speed of decline is the story.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Poverty lines are thresholds; moving just above them does not mean security.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Line chart with clear yearly markers.

chartExplainers.title: The all-in human scorecard
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's HDI reached 0.69 in 2023, on par with Bangladesh, but South Korea's was 0.94.
chartExplainers.detail: The Human Development Index from 1990 to 2023. India moved from 0.45 to 0.69. China went from 0.49 to 0.80. South Korea from 0.74 to 0.94. Vietnam from 0.50 to 0.77. Bangladesh from 0.40 to 0.69, tying India. The index combines life expectancy, schooling, and income. India's score is pulled down by lower health and education components relative to its income. Vietnam, with a lower per capita income, has a higher HDI due to better health and schooling.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: A composite that captures the balanced development East Asia achieved and India missed.
chartExplainers.howToRead: See the clustering: India and Bangladesh at the bottom, Korea at the top.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: HDI is a broad average and hides inequality within countries.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Line chart with three key data points per country.

chartExplainers.title: The road not taken: democracy
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's electoral democracy index was 0.38 in 2025, while China's was 0.07, and South Korea democratised late.
chartExplainers.detail: The V-Dem electoral democracy index from 1900 to 2025. India's score rose to around 0.4-0.5 after independence and stood at 0.38 in 2025, indicating a continuous but imperfect electoral democracy. China's score stayed near 0.06-0.07. South Korea was below 0.3 until the late 1980s, then jumped to 0.82. Taiwan followed a similar path, reaching 0.80 after the 1990s. So India maintained democratic politics while East Asia's takeoffs occurred under authoritarian rule.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The honest counterweight: democracy may have a growth cost, but it avoids coercion.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the timing: Korea and Taiwan's scores rise only after they were already rich.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This index measures electoral competition, not governance quality or rights.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Simple line chart with vertical lines marking regime changes.

sourceNotes: Maddison Project 2023 for long-run GDP per capita (2011 int-$)

World Bank for GDP per capita at PPP (constant 2021 int-$), life expectancy, under-five mortality, fertility, stunting, gross fixed capital formation, gross savings, FDI, manufacturing value added, sector value added, employment, manufactures exports, R&D, electricity consumption, urban population
Harvard Growth Lab Atlas of Economic Complexity for the Economic Complexity Index
Penn World Table via Our World in Data for output per hour worked
UNDP via Our World in Data for the Human Development Index
V-Dem via Our World in Data for the electoral democracy index
ILO modelled estimates for female labour force participation and employment by sector
World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform via Our World in Data for $3/day poverty
caveats: Maddison Project figures before 1950 are reconstructions with wide error bars; read as broad trajectories, not precise levels.
PPP figures are modelled price adjustments, not market exchange rates; never mix with market-rate dollars.
Female labour force participation and employment-by-sector figures are ILO modelled estimates that can differ from national surveys; much women's work is informal and undercounted.
Taiwan is absent from World Bank and UN datasets; its data comes only from Maddison, Penn World Table, and V-Dem.
China's World Bank manufacturing value added series begins only in 2004, so it is omitted from the manufacturing-share chart.
Singapore and Hong Kong are not included as manufacturing comparators because their trade-to-GDP is inflated by re-exports.
Several factors moving together in these charts does not prove any single one caused the divergence; causation is tangled.

plannedCharts.title: The same starting line, and the great divergence
plannedCharts.subtitle: Maddison Project 2023 · GDP per capita in 2011 int-$ · a long-run reconstruction, read as broad trajectory not precise levels
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Around 1950 India was no poorer than its Asian peers; this is the gap that opened up afterward.

plannedCharts.title: The income gap, measured
plannedCharts.unit: GDP per capita at PPP (constant 2021 int-$)
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · GDP per capita at purchasing-power parity, constant 2021 international dollars
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The measured, modern version of the gap, on a like-for-like PPP basis.

plannedCharts.title: How long people live
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · life expectancy at birth · 1960 to latest
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: East Asia invested in survival first; the life-expectancy gap opened decades before the income gap.

plannedCharts.title: Children who don't reach five
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · under-five mortality · 1960 to latest
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A healthy, surviving child is the first input into a future workforce.

plannedCharts.title: Years of schooling, the long view
plannedCharts.subtitle: Lee-Lee / Barro-Lee via Our World in Data · average years of schooling, adults · the human-capital stock, not just enrolment
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Schooling is the human-capital stock a country brings to industrialisation; India started this climb late and slow.

plannedCharts.title: Where are the women at work?
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / ILO modelled estimate · share of women 15+ in the labour force
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's single most striking outlier: barely a quarter to a third of women are in the measured workforce, far below its Asian peers.

plannedCharts.title: How fast families shrank
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · total fertility rate · 1960 to latest
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Faster fertility decline opened the demographic-dividend window earlier in East Asia.

plannedCharts.title: Children too short for their age
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · child stunting, latest available year per country
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Stunting is malnutrition written into a child's body and brain; India's rate is high even against much poorer countries.

plannedCharts.title: How much each country built
plannedCharts.unit: gross fixed capital formation (% of GDP)
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · gross fixed capital formation as a share of GDP · the investment rate
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The under-told engine of the miracle: East Asia poured a huge share of output into factories, roads and machines.

plannedCharts.title: Who saved to pay for it
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · gross savings as a share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: High investment was financed by high domestic saving; the two move together.

plannedCharts.title: Foreign money coming in
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · foreign direct investment, net inflows, as a share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Foreign factories brought capital, technology and export markets; Vietnam and China pulled in far more, relative to their size.

plannedCharts.title: The factory escalator India never rode
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · manufacturing value added as a share of GDP · India and South Korea have full records; China's WB series starts only in 2004
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Manufacturing is the escalator out of poverty; Korea's share humped up and stayed, India's never really rose.

plannedCharts.title: India's leap from farm to office
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · India's value added by sector · the structural leap that skipped the factory
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's workers and output moved from farms to services, largely skipping the mass-manufacturing stage in between.

plannedCharts.title: Who left the farm, and when
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / ILO modelled estimate · share of workers in agriculture
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Development means moving workers off the farm into higher-productivity work; India did this slower than its peers.

plannedCharts.title: Climbing the complexity ladder
plannedCharts.subtitle: Harvard Growth Lab Atlas of Economic Complexity · how diverse and sophisticated each country's exports are
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: It is not just how much you export but what: Korea and China climbed to complex, high-value goods while India's basket stayed simpler.

plannedCharts.title: Making things to sell the world
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · manufactured goods as a share of merchandise exports
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Export-led growth runs on selling manufactures to the world; the peers built that engine, India leaned more on services and commodities.

plannedCharts.title: How much each worker produces
plannedCharts.subtitle: Penn World Table via Our World in Data · real GDP per hour worked
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The income gap is, at bottom, a productivity gap: how much value an hour of work creates.

plannedCharts.title: Spending on inventing the future
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · gross domestic expenditure on research and development as a share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Korea now spends a world-leading share on R&D; India's has been stuck below one percent for decades.

plannedCharts.title: The power to run a factory
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · electric power consumption per person · a proxy for industrial and household capacity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Power per person tracks how industrial and how comfortable a society has become.

plannedCharts.title: Moving to the city
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · share of population living in urban areas
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Factories and services cluster in cities; East Asia urbanised fast, India more slowly and messily.

plannedCharts.title: The poverty India did crush
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform via Our World in Data · share below the $3-a-day extreme-poverty line
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's genuine win: hundreds of millions lifted out of extreme poverty, even without the manufacturing boom.

plannedCharts.title: The all-in human scorecard
plannedCharts.subtitle: UNDP via Our World in Data · Human Development Index, combining health, schooling and income
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A single composite of health, schooling and income, to see the whole gap at once.

plannedCharts.title: The road not taken: democracy
plannedCharts.subtitle: V-Dem via Our World in Data · electoral democracy index · India stayed democratic; Korea and Taiwan democratised only after their growth takeoff
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The honest counterweight: India built its record under continuous democracy, while the East Asian transformations were often coerced and only democratised later.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
